paragraphs.body: The car comes to a stop, my heart is pounding out of my chest. I glance at my mom, my voice barely above a whisper. "Do I really have to go?" My words tremble as if I already don’t know the answer. “Yes, you have to go” she says, her voice is calm and steady, trying to soothe me. There's nothing to worry about. Everyone will love you” my mom said, attempting to ease my anxiety. In the most teenage way possible, I roll my eyes at her. "Sure, Mom. You have to say that. You’re my mom”. She sighs and looks at her watch. “Come on, it's almost 7:40am and you don’t want to be late on your first day of high school”. I shrug, I put on my face mask.The cool air outside hits me like a slap in the face as I open the car door. The weather is cold and dry. I glance at my phone. November 9th 2020, my first day of freshman year. This is the third time now I've moved states and restarted at a new school but this time it's worse. Not only am I the new kid but I've been isolated the past year because of the covid pandemic. "Do I even remember how to talk to people?" I think to myself as my anxiety builds. My heart is pounding, my hands are slick with sweat even though it's cold outside. Everything feels wrong, I'm scared, I'm shaking. I try to ease myself so people around me don't think I'm crazy. I take a deep breath and adjust my mask. I glance down at my outfit, “ i like my outfit” I tell myself and for a second, I almost feel confident. but then my thoughts spiral. "What if no one else likes my outfit? And my shoes! Why did I wear these shoes? I always trip in these shoes. I’m going to walk into my first class and fall flat on my face. Everyone’s going to laugh." my breathing quickens. I turn around trying to find an escape, I begin to panic more. Where is my mom? Did She leave me already? I start to freak out. But then I spotted her. She's parked waiting to make sure I get inside. I start walking back to the car but then I see her through the windshield and begin to shake her head. She picks in her phone and begins typing away. DING. my phone goes off. I look at my phone already knowing it was from her.

paragraphs.body: “You’re stronger than you think. Just be yourself. You got this. I love you” I read the message and I started to feel comfort. I looked down and see the necklace hanging around my neck. The necklace pendant my mom got me. I hold it and smile. The nerves are all still there but knowing my mom has my back gave me this tremendous feeling of reassurance. I smiled at her and turned around to face the school. There's no turning back now. I take my first step into a whole new world.

In a world filled with so much uncertainty, momentum most often acts as the driving force that pushes us forward, especially during times when we feel like backing out. For me, momentum is not just a word but an important feeling. a Feeling I needed on that first day of high school. It represents and symbolizes both a physical and emotional force, urging me to keep moving despite any challenges and doubts I face. This force is frequently fueled by the determination to keep going even when everything seems to be against me. Sometimes, it becomes a metaphor that helps me better understand myself and my experiences.

paragraphs.body: As I stepped into that high school on my first day, I could feel a wave of momentum pushing me forward. After getting that reassuring text from my mom and holding onto my pendant, the nervous energy I had been having started to change from an obstacle into a fuel that would push me into the unknown. This feeling wasn’t entirely new to me. I moved states multiple different times growing up and I learned pretty quickly how to restart something new, make friends, build connections, and adjust to different environments. However, this time was different. The isolation and quarantines brought on by the pandemic made the process even more difficult. I felt as if I had almost completely forgotten what it felt like to engage with others, and that first day felt like a big challenge to overcome. Yet, as the moments went by, I realized that momentum was what enabled me to take that crucial first step.

paragraphs.body: In many ways, my necklace pendant my mom gifted me expresses this very idea of momentum. The same pendant that provided strength for me for many hard months. The pendant is small, about the size of a quarter, and is a sterling silver. Its coolness is subtly noticeable when I touch it, and one side is engraved with the words “EVIL EYE” in delicate script. The opposite side has a more intricate design, with swirls and patterns that feel almost tangible beneath my fingers. The pendant has a raised iris, with a blue sapphire at its center that resembles a beautiful color almost like a piece of the sky. The oxidized silver gives it a darker appearance, as if it has endured the passage of time, weathered yet still shining in its unique way. When I smell the pendant it smells like metal but also has a slight smell of my perfume that I spray on my neck that hits the necklace too. It is a bigger heavier pendant so I feel a slight weight to it when I wear it around my neck. The pendant is something so precious to me and proves a strong sense of comfort and always reminds me to keep going.

annotations.comment: I'm still very interested in this detail. :)

paragraphs.body: Momentum is often defined as the force something possesses while in motion. However, to me, it represents more than just speed. It represents the ability to push forward in the face of fear and hesitation. The term itself originates from the Latin word "movere," which means "to move." Yet, momentum isn't solely about physical movement. At times, it reflects emotional resilience. For me, momentum was the invisible driving force that enabled me to push through my doubt and step into that school on my first day, despite my intense anxiety. It was what pushed me forward one step at a time into a world that felt very unfamiliar and intimidating.

The pendant to me serves as a powerful symbol of momentum. Just as its weight remains constant, so does the inner strength we possess, often without even realizing it. It reminds me that in times of fear and uncertainty, I have the capacity to push ahead. Reflecting on my first day at school, I had to gather up the courage to continue, to take that important step despite the overwhelming unknown. Similarly, the pendant hangs around my neck, embodying the force of momentum that propels me forward when I feel hesitant. When doubt creeps in my pendant reminds me of my strength and the momentum that got me through the first day. Work Cited “Online Etymology Dictionary.” Etymonline, www.etymonline.com/..
